resources.string: Split a secret into shards. A quorum reconstructs it.

ShardQuorum protects a single high-value secret - a password-manager passphrase, a crypto wallet seed, a disk-encryption key - by splitting it into pieces called shards. You decide how many shards exist and how many are needed to rebuild the secret. The app works entirely offline and never touches the internet.

The idea behind ShardQuorum is Shamir\'s Secret Sharing. It splits your secret into a number of shards (N) and sets a quorum (K). Any K shards rebuild the secret exactly. Any K minus 1 or fewer reveal nothing at all - not part of the secret, not even a hint. This is a mathematical guarantee, not just a hard puzzle.\n\nA common choice is 3-of-5: five shards exist, any three rebuild the secret. You could lose two shards, or have two fall into the wrong hands, and your secret stays both recoverable and safe.

Each shard is encoded using SSKR (Sharded Secret Key Reconstruction), an open standard from Blockchain Commons. A shard can be kept two ways:

• As a QR code, for quick scanning.
• As a short list of plain English words, easy to write on paper by hand.

Both carry the same information. The words include a built-in checksum, so a typo is caught before it can cause trouble.

By default your secret travels with the shards as an encrypted recovery envelope. Keep a copy of the envelope alongside every shard. The shards unlock the envelope; on their own, neither the shards nor the envelope reveal anything. (If you pick Direct mode - for example for a wallet seed - there is no envelope, and the shards are the secret itself.)
Create. Tap “Create a new secret”, give it a vague name, type or paste your secret, and choose your quorum (K) and number of shards (N). The app generates the shards and shows them one at a time.

Distribute. Save or share each shard separately, and store them in different places with different people - a fireproof box at home, a trusted relative, a bank safe-deposit box. No single place should hold enough shards to reach the quorum.
Verify. Before you rely on them, the app asks you to re-enter a quorum of shards to prove they really do rebuild the secret. Do not skip this step.
Recover. When you need the secret back, tap “Recover from shards” and add a quorum of them by scanning QR codes, typing the words, or importing a saved file.

Send each shard a different way. If one app, account, or person ends up holding enough shards, they can rebuild your secret on their own.\n\nWhen the secret is split or rebuilt, the phone briefly sees the whole thing. ShardQuorum protects your secret while it is stored and distributed, not while it is in use - so do this on a device you trust. The screen also blocks screenshots while shards are shown.

Your shards use SSKR, an open standard, so other SSKR-compatible tools can read them - you are not locked into this app.\n\nIn Direct mode, another tool rebuilds your secret directly. In the default encrypted-envelope mode, other tools can recombine the shards but recover only the random key, not your secret - the envelope that holds the secret is ShardQuorum\'s own format. So for recovery that never depends on this app, either use Direct mode (for a 16-32 byte secret such as a wallet seed), or keep a copy of ShardQuorum stored alongside your shards.
Add your first shard to begin.
Type the words printed under a shard\'s QR code, separated by spaces.
“%1$s” is not a valid word - did you mean: %2$s?
Point the camera at a shard\'s QR code - it is added automatically. Scan each shard (and the envelope QR in encrypted mode).
Camera access is used only to scan shard QR codes, on-device. It is never required - you can also type words or import a file.
Import a saved shard file: a QR image (PNG) or a saved words text file.
Not a recognized shard or envelope.
That shard belongs to a different secret.
Add more shards to reach the quorum.
These shards do not combine into a valid secret. They may be wrong, too few, or damaged.
The shards combined, but the recovery envelope is wrong, missing, or damaged.
Could not find a QR code in that image. Try a clearer photo or type the words instead.
Re-enter any %1$d shards the way recipients will - scan a QR, type the words, or import a file - to prove they rebuild this secret before you distribute them.
Verified - these shards rebuild your secret.
These shards do NOT rebuild the secret. Re-check what you saved, then try again.
That shard is from a different secret.
Without verifying, you cannot be sure your saved shards actually rebuild the secret. If any were mis-saved, the secret may be lost. Skip anyway?
Shown only while this screen is open. It is not saved.

This may be an encrypted secret\'s key, not the secret itself. If you created it with a recovery envelope (the default), add that envelope to decrypt. If you used direct mode (e.g. a wallet seed), the value below is your secret.
Shown on every shard - anyone who has or finds one can read it, so keep it vague.
Any text secret. A random key is split into shards; the secret travels alongside them as an encrypted envelope. The secret can be changed later without redistributing shards.
Hex secret of 16-32 bytes (even length), split directly as standard SSKR. Recoverable by any SSKR-compatible tool. Wallet seeds fit this mode.
Any %1$d of %2$d shards reconstruct the secret. Fewer reveal nothing.
Direct mode needs 16-32 bytes (32-64 hex digits, even byte count).
Any %1$d of %2$d shards reconstruct the secret
These shards are shown only once and will be discarded. You will need to start over. Leave anyway?
This shard plus the offline recovery tool and spec. Recommended.
This shard plus the offline recovery tool and spec. Recommended.

Send each shard a different way, to a different person or place. If one app, account, or person ends up holding enough shards, they can rebuild your secret.\n\nSharing hands this shard to another app, which may back it up or sync it to the cloud. Email and text messages send it in clear text. When you can, choose “Save to file” or an offline transfer instead.
Keep a copy of this envelope with every shard. Shards alone cannot recover the secret; the envelope alone reveals nothing.
QR code of the encrypted recovery envelope
